company.name: eBay
company.description: eBay operates a global online marketplace connecting buyers and sellers across consumer goods, collectibles, auto parts, vehicles, and other categories.

overview.title: Marketplace at scale
paragraphs: eBay is a mature two-sided commerce marketplace whose revenue is primarily derived from take-rate economics on gross merchandise volume and seller advertising.
The company reported nearly $80 billion of 2025 GMV, 135 million active buyers, and 2.5 billion live listings globally, giving it deep category breadth and liquidity.

overview.title: Trust and category tooling
paragraphs: eBay's moat is not just listing volume; it also includes payments, search, seller reputation, buyer protection, dispute workflows, advertising tools, and category-specific experiences.
eBay Motors extends the marketplace model into vehicles, parts, and accessories with fitment search, vehicle history reports, purchase-protection workflows, and dealer connections.

moatNarrative: eBay has a strong marketplace moat because buyers come for selection and price discovery while sellers come for demand, tooling, and established trust infrastructure. Its scale in collectibles, refurbished goods, auto parts, and long-tail inventory is difficult for a blank-slate marketplace to replicate quickly.

The moat is weaker than fully vertically integrated retail because listings, sellers, logistics, and buyer relationships are only partly controlled by eBay. The same openness that makes eBay broad also leaves room for category specialists, social commerce, direct seller storefronts, and decentralized tools to peel off niches.
decentralizationNarrative: A peer-to-peer marketplace is conceptually decentralizable: listings, buyer-seller messaging, escrow, reputation, and dispute resolution can be moved from a central marketplace operator toward protocols and federated or blockchain-assisted networks.

The hard part is not publishing listings; it is bootstrapping liquidity, search quality, fraud controls, moderation, shipping accountability, and dispute resolution. That makes eBay vulnerable to decentralized pressure in ideologically aligned or high-fee niches first, while its broad mainstream marketplace remains comparatively resilient.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 201-225.

moat.rationale: eBay combines large-scale buyer demand, seller supply, global listing density, reputation systems, payments, advertising, and category tooling, though it faces intense competition from other online and offline commerce channels.

decentralizability.rationale: The core marketplace primitives are naturally peer-to-peer, and existing decentralized marketplace projects show feasible listing, messaging, escrow, and dispute primitives, but mainstream liquidity and trust operations remain hard to decentralize.

profitability.rationale: eBay's take-rate and advertising model generated substantial revenue from nearly $80 billion of 2025 GMV, with the company remaining a mature profitable marketplace business.

peRatio.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported eBay's trailing P/E ratio at about 24.1 as of May 2026.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported eBay's market capitalization at $48.51 billion as of June 2026.

products.name: eBay marketplace
products.summary: eBay's core marketplace lets consumers and businesses list, discover, buy, sell, and advertise goods across a broad range of categories.
products.whyItMatters: The marketplace is eBay's central economic engine and a large example of centralized coordination over otherwise peer-to-peer commerce.
replacementSketch: A credible replacement would not need to clone all of eBay at once. It could begin with collector communities, used electronics, repair parts, privacy-sensitive goods, or local trade where sellers accept more operational burden in exchange for lower platform dependence.

The lightweight version is an interoperable marketplace stack: portable seller identity, signed listings, encrypted buyer-seller messaging, escrow, reputation export, and multiple discovery front ends.

alternatives.name: OpenBazaar
alternatives.summary: OpenBazaar is an open-source decentralized marketplace project using peer-to-peer commerce, Bitcoin payments, multisignature escrow, encrypted chat, and open dispute moderation.

openness.rationale: The project is openly published on GitHub and documents open marketplace primitives such as listings, chat, Bitcoin payments, and escrow.

decentralizationFit.rationale: OpenBazaar was designed specifically around peer-to-peer marketplace coordination rather than a central marketplace operator.

readiness.rationale: The architecture is relevant, but current ecosystem activity and mainstream usability appear far below eBay-scale production readiness.

costLeverage.rationale: Direct peer-to-peer payments and reduced payment-processor dependence could lower transaction friction, though liquidity and support costs would move to participants or service providers.

alternatives.name: Particl Marketplace
alternatives.summary: Particl Marketplace is an open-source decentralized marketplace focused on private peer-to-peer e-commerce, decentralized messaging, and escrow without a central intermediary.

openness.rationale: Particl publishes its marketplace and core software through public repositories and describes the code as open source.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The marketplace is explicitly built for decentralized person-to-person e-commerce using peer-to-peer messaging and blockchain-based settlement tools.

readiness.rationale: Particl has downloadable software and maintained repositories, but it lacks the mainstream liquidity, support footprint, and buyer familiarity of a large marketplace.

costLeverage.rationale: Removing platform commissions and third-party escrow could reduce fees for committed participants, while self-custody and discovery costs remain real adoption barriers.

disruptionConcepts.name: Portable reputation peer-to-peer marketplace
disruptionConcepts.summary: A decentralized commerce network could separate listings, search, reputation, escrow, and dispute resolution into interoperable components so sellers are not locked into one marketplace and buyers can use competing discovery clients.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: eBay's central take-rate moat weakens if sellers can publish signed listings once, carry reputation across interfaces, and settle through escrow instead of relying on a single marketplace database.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Bitcoin multisignature escrow and decentralized identity primitives can coordinate payment release without giving a marketplace operator custody or unilateral control over seller access.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Sellers publish signed listings to peer-to-peer or federated indexes, buyers discover them through competing clients, and independent moderators or escrow agents compete on reputation and fees.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Trade funds are held in multisignature escrow, seller history is tied to portable keys, buyer feedback is signed, and moderators can only resolve disputes when selected by the trading parties before the transaction.
failureModes: Liquidity may remain too thin outside niche categories.
Sybil reputation attacks and fake reviews can still distort search if identity costs are too low.
Moderation and illegal-goods enforcement are harder without a central operator.
adoptionPath: Start with high-fee collector or repair-part communities that already tolerate specialized workflows.
Add import tools for seller catalogs and reputation proofs from existing platforms.
Let search clients, moderators, and escrow services compete while preserving the same listing and identity layer.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept directly decomposes a centralized marketplace into peer-to-peer listings, portable identity, escrow, and independent moderation.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: OpenBazaar and Particl demonstrate credible primitives, but large-scale reputation, search ranking, and dispute governance remain unresolved at mainstream scale.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The technical pieces exist, but product polish, fraud response, liquidity incentives, and legal compliance make implementation materially harder than a protocol demo.

incumbentPressure.rationale: This would pressure take rates and seller lock-in in niche categories first, but eBay's buyer liquidity and protection infrastructure would remain strong defenses.

products.name: eBay Motors
products.summary: eBay Motors is eBay's marketplace experience for buying and selling vehicles, parts, and accessories with fitment search, bidding, fixed-price listings, vehicle reports, and purchase-protection workflows.
products.whyItMatters: Motors shows how eBay's marketplace model becomes more defensible when trust, compatibility data, fraud prevention, financing, deposits, and offline fulfillment matter.
replacementSketch: A replacement would likely begin around parts and repair rather than full-vehicle transactions, where standardized fitment records, seller reputation, and local pickup can be coordinated by smaller networks.

For vehicles, an open protocol could coordinate listings, inspection attestations, title checks, deposits, and local dealer or mechanic verification without requiring one central marketplace to own the whole buyer journey.

alternatives.name: Particl Marketplace
alternatives.summary: Particl Marketplace can serve as a decentralized commerce substrate for physical goods, though it would need category-specific fitment, inspection, and logistics layers to replace eBay Motors.

openness.rationale: Particl's public repositories and documentation support open-source marketplace software.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The underlying peer-to-peer commerce model fits auto parts better than full vehicles because smaller shippable goods need less offline verification.

readiness.rationale: Particl does not appear to provide the vehicle fitment, inspection, purchase-protection, or dealer workflows expected in a dedicated motors marketplace.

costLeverage.rationale: Decentralized commerce could reduce listing and escrow fees, but auto transactions still require paid verification, shipping, inspection, returns, or title services.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open auto parts fitment network
disruptionConcepts.summary: An open network for auto parts could combine standardized fitment data, signed seller listings, buyer vehicle profiles, and escrowed payments so parts sellers can reach buyers without depending on a single marketplace interface.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: eBay Motors' auto-parts advantage depends heavily on compatibility search and buyer trust; an open fitment and reputation layer would make those advantages portable across independent storefronts.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through portable signed listings, shared fitment attestations, and escrowed settlement rather than through a token-first marketplace.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Parts sellers publish listings with machine-readable compatibility data, buyers match against saved vehicle profiles, and independent front ends index the same signed inventory.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Fitment claims are backed by seller-signed data, return outcomes, buyer confirmations, and optional mechanic or recycler attestations; escrow releases only after delivery or dispute-window expiration.
failureModes: Fitment data can be wrong, incomplete, or intentionally gamed.
Returns for heavy or used parts can erase fee savings.
Independent indexes may fragment discovery before a shared reputation layer matures.
adoptionPath: Start with enthusiast communities, salvage yards, and repair shops that already maintain structured parts knowledge.
Publish open fitment schemas and importers for seller inventory systems.
Add escrow, return attestations, and reputation portability once listing liquidity exists.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Auto parts are well suited to peer-to-peer inventory discovery if fitment and seller reputation become portable.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The coordination path is credible for parts listings, but reliable fitment data and returns governance are non-trivial.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The marketplace primitives exist, while category-specific data standards, seller tooling, and inspection workflows would require focused ecosystem work.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept can pressure fees and lock-in in enthusiast and recycler niches, but eBay's search scale, buyer protection, and existing inventory remain strong advantages.
